title: Systems of Care & the Chain of Survival
learningObjectives: Describe how ACLS providers extend and strengthen every link of the 2025 AHA universal Chain of Survival.
Differentiate the systems-of-care emphasis for out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA) versus in-hospital cardiac arrest (IHCA).
Explain the rationale for regionalized cardiac arrest centers and post-arrest transfer.
Describe continuous quality improvement (CQI) and Utstein-style reporting as tools for system performance.
Explain why post-cardiac arrest care is a continuation of resuscitation rather than an afterthought.
Describe the multidisciplinary, team-based nature of an ACLS resuscitation.
Identify the role of early-warning scores and rapid-response teams in preventing in-hospital arrest.

summary.title: From BLS to ACLS: Extending the Chain
summary.content: ACLS builds directly on BLS. The first links of the 2025 universal Chain of Survival — recognition, activation, high-quality CPR, and early defibrillation — remain the strongest predictors of survival, and ACLS providers must protect them even while adding advanced airway management, IV/IO access, rhythm-specific therapy, and drug administration. Advanced interventions never replace high-quality compressions and early defibrillation; they are layered on top of a BLS foundation that cannot be interrupted for long.

summary.title: The 2025 Universal Chain of Survival — the ACLS Provider's View
summary.content: The six-link universal chain (activation, high-quality CPR, defibrillation, advanced resuscitation interventions, post-cardiac arrest care, recovery) applies across all settings, with a separate Neonatal Chain of Care for newborns. ACLS providers are the primary owners of link 4 (advanced resuscitation interventions: airway, rhythm therapy, medications, treatment of reversible causes) and share ownership of link 5 (post-cardiac arrest care) with critical-care and cardiology teams. A weak link anywhere in the chain — including a delayed or interrupted advanced intervention — degrades the whole system's performance.

summary.title: Systems of Care: OHCA versus IHCA
summary.content: Out-of-hospital systems depend on bystander CPR, dispatcher-assisted instructions, public-access AEDs, and EMS response times; ACLS-level care begins when advanced providers arrive and continues through transport and hospital hand-off. In-hospital systems emphasize surveillance and prevention — continuous monitoring, early-warning scores, and rapid-response or medical emergency teams — so many arrests are recognized and treated before a full arrest occurs. Both systems converge on the same standard once ACLS-level care begins: high-quality CPR, minimal interruptions, and rhythm-appropriate therapy.

summary.title: Regionalized Cardiac Arrest Centers
summary.content: Evidence increasingly supports triaging resuscitated (or refractory) cardiac arrest patients to specialized cardiac arrest centers capable of 24/7 coronary angiography/PCI, targeted temperature management, multimodal neuroprognostication, and ECPR when indicated, analogous to trauma and stroke systems of care. Where such regionalized systems exist, protocols should route appropriate post-ROSC and refractory-arrest patients to these centers rather than the nearest facility alone.

summary.title: Continuous Quality Improvement & Utstein Reporting
summary.content: High-performing resuscitation systems measure what they do: chest compression fraction, time to first shock, time to epinephrine, ROSC rate, survival to discharge, and neurologic outcome, using standardized Utstein-style templates so performance can be compared across systems and over time. Debriefing after real events (and after simulation), combined with data-driven feedback, is how teams and systems improve — CQI is itself a component of the chain, not a separate add-on.

summary.title: Post-Cardiac Arrest Care as Continued Resuscitation
summary.content: The 2025 guidelines emphasize that post-cardiac arrest care is a direct continuation of resuscitation, not a separate phase that begins after ROSC. The same team mindset — anticipate deterioration, treat reversible causes, communicate in closed loops — carries forward into airway/ventilation management, hemodynamic support, temperature control, urgent coronary evaluation, and early planning for neuroprognostication and recovery.

clinicalPearls: Advanced interventions are additive, not substitutive — they must never come at the cost of compression fraction or time to first shock.
In-hospital systems win by preventing arrest (surveillance, early-warning scores); out-of-hospital systems win by compressing the time from collapse to CPR and shock.
Consider regionalized cardiac arrest center transfer for post-ROSC and refractory-arrest patients where systems of care support it.
What isn't measured isn't improved — CQI metrics (CCF, time-to-shock, time-to-epinephrine) are core system components, not paperwork.
redFlags: Advanced procedures (intubation, IV/IO attempts) causing long pauses in chest compressions.
Treating ROSC as the end of the resuscitation rather than the start of the post-arrest phase.
No local process for identifying candidates for cardiac arrest center transfer or CQI review.
commonMistakes: Prioritizing advanced airway placement over compression quality and early defibrillation.
Assuming systems-of-care improvement is an administrative task unrelated to bedside providers.
Failing to debrief after an arrest, losing the chance to identify system gaps.
memoryAids: 'BLS first, ACLS on top' — advanced care layers onto, never replaces, compressions and defibrillation.
OHCA = bystanders and speed; IHCA = surveillance and prevention.
Measure it to improve it — CCF, time-to-shock, time-to-epi, survival, neuro outcome.

decisionTrees.title: ACLS Provider's Place in the System of Care
decisionTrees.content:
Cardiac arrest recognized (OHCA or IHCA)
  → Link 1: Activation — call for help / activate resuscitation team
  → Link 2: High-quality CPR — protect this throughout everything that follows
  → Link 3: Defibrillation — shock a shockable rhythm ASAP, resume compressions immediately
  → Link 4 (ACLS provider's primary role): advanced airway when indicated, IV/IO access, rhythm-specific drugs, treat H's & T's
  → ROSC achieved
     → Link 5: Post-cardiac arrest care — airway/ventilation, hemodynamics, 12-lead ECG, temperature control, consider cardiac arrest center transfer
  → Link 6: Recovery — neuroprognostication timeline, rehabilitation, survivor and family support
  → System feedback: CQI review / Utstein-style data feeds back into training and protocols

references: AHA 2025 Guidelines for CPR and ECC — Executive Summary and Systems of Care
AHA 2025 Guidelines for CPR and ECC — Part 9: Adult Advanced Life Support
AHA 2025 Guidelines for CPR and ECC — Part 11: Post-Cardiac Arrest Care
